g.data-narration: Welcome to Dynamic Memory Allocation in C. Learn the four essential functions for flexible memory management.
g.text: Dynamic Memory Allocation Functions in C

g.data-narration: Dynamic memory allocation lets programs request memory at runtime from the heap, unlike static allocation at compile time.
g.text: Enables flexible data structures that grow/shrink as needed

g.data-narration: This is crucial for implementing linked lists, trees, graphs, and dynamic arrays that adapt to varying data sizes.
malloc allocates a block of specified bytes. Returns pointer to uninitialized memory or NULL if allocation fails.
Here's an example: allocating space for 5 integers. Always check for NULL to handle allocation failures safely.
calloc allocates memory for an array and initializes all bytes to zero. Slower than malloc but safer for arrays.
calloc automatically initializes memory to zero. Perfect for arrays where you want all elements starting at zero.
realloc resizes existing memory blocks. May move data to a new location. Preserves old content up to minimum of old and new sizes.
Example: resize array from 5 to 10 elements. The returned pointer may be different from the original.
free deallocates memory, making it available for future allocations. Always set pointer to NULL after freeing to avoid dangling pointers.
Key differences: malloc takes 1 arg and is faster; calloc takes 2 args, initializes to zero; realloc resizes existing blocks.
g.text: Comparison: malloc vs calloc vs realloc

g.data-narration: Three critical errors to avoid: memory leaks (forgot to free), dangling pointers (use after free), and double free errors.
Always check return values for NULL, match every malloc with free, and set pointers to NULL after freeing.
Key takeaways: malloc for single blocks, calloc for zero-initialized arrays, realloc to resize, free to release. Match every allocation with deallocation!
